title: Detecting Cycles in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

statement.markdown: Given a DAG represented as an adjacency list, implement a function to detect whether the graph contains a cycle. The function should return a boolean value indicating whether a cycle exists.
statement.inputFormat: An adjacency list representation of a DAG, where each entry 'i' is a list of integers representing the nodes that node 'i' is connected to.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether a cycle exists in the graph.
statement.notes: The graph is represented as a list of lists, where each inner list represents a node and the integers within the list represent the nodes that the current node is connected to.

hints.text: Check if the adjacency list contains any redundant connections.
Consider a depth-first search to traverse the graph and detect cycles.

editorial.title: Detecting Cycles in a DAG
editorial.intuition: A cycle in a DAG can be detected using a depth-first search (DFS) traversal. If a node has already been visited and is revisited during the traversal, it indicates a cycle.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach involves checking for cycles by traversing the graph in all possible paths, which is time-consuming and inefficient for large graphs.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach involves using a cycle detection algorithm, such as the DFS traversal with a stack and a set to keep track of visited nodes.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a cycle detection algorithm that can detect cycles in O(V+E) time complexity, where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the algorithm can be proven by showing that if a cycle exists, the algorithm will detect it, and if no cycle exists, the algorithm will correctly indicate that.
editorial.dryRun: To illustrate the dry-run, consider the graph [1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[4,1]. Start the traversal from node 1. The DFS will follow the connections: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 1. Since node 1 has already been visited, it indicates a cycle.
commonMistakes: Not keeping track of visited nodes
